description: An observation or record may be offered as 'thesori' (evidence) for or against a claim. Measurements, traces, and testimony can enter the same role. The noun describes how that material enters an assessment, not whether it proves the point; two speakers may accept the evidence and still disagree about its relevance or interpretation.
articulatory_notes: Rest the tongue tip at the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th' and release the breath into middle-height front 'e'. Bring the tongue blade close to the alveolar ridge for 's', then round into stressed middle-height back 'o'. Finish with an alveolar tap or brief trill followed by high-front 'i'.
usage_notes: In context, evidence may form a body of material or appear as one distinct item. 'hi', 'ke', 'ti', and 'ho' mark the claimed source of a verb; they do not certify the quality of evidence named in the sentence. Method, provenance, uncertainty, and limits belong nearby when they affect the claim.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Evidence lets a speaker return from conviction to what was observed, recorded, or reported. The interpretation can then change without pretending the earlier evidence never existed.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Skilled observation, testimony, tracks, weather signs, and embodied records can all enter an inquiry as evidence. Numerical instruments are one route among several, not the definition of knowing.

semantic_domains.cognition: material considered for what it supports or counts against
semantic_domains.communication: material offered so that a claim can be assessed by others
semantic_domains.wisdom: support whose source, relevance, and limits remain open to examination
